# Figure description: Regional Mullock Sampling, Fig 10.png

**Generated by**: claude-sonnet-4-6

---

## Figure Type
Satellite imagery base map with geochemical sample overlay — specifically a mullock heap sampling results map showing gold (Au) assay values plotted as colour-coded point symbols over a high-resolution aerial/satellite photograph.

## Coverage and Subject
The map covers an area of approximately 8–10 km north-south by 6–8 km east-west in the Murchison region of Western Australia, centred on and north of the town of **Coe**. Named locations visible include:
- **Coe** (town, central-right)
- **Great Cuchulainn Pit** (lower-central)
- **South Cuchulainn** (lower-central)
- **Veldt** (lower-right)

A large open-pit mining operation is visible in the upper-left of the image. White polygons delineate what appear to be **tenement boundaries** (mining lease or exploration licence blocks) covering most of the central corridor of the map. A scale bar indicates approximately **0–2 km**.

## Key Content
The figure displays **mullock sampling results in ppm gold (Au)** from historical mullock heaps (waste rock dumps from old workings), classified into four categories shown as diamond-shaped symbols:

- **Cyan diamonds (0–0.4 ppm Au):** Low-grade results; scattered across multiple locations including the southern cluster and central corridor
- **Green diamonds (0.4–0.8 ppm Au):** Moderate-low grade; present in the east-central cluster and southern grouping
- **Yellow diamonds (0.8–1.6 ppm Au):** Moderate-high grade; notable occurrences in the upper-central area and the east-central cluster
- **Red diamonds (1.6–123 ppm Au):** High-grade results; the most significant category, with concentrations notably in:
  - The **upper-central area** (northernmost cluster, ~3–4 km north of Coe)
  - The **east-central cluster** (right-centre of map, east of the main tenement corridor)
  - The **southern cluster** near the bottom-left of the map

The highest-grade results (up to **123 ppm Au**) are found primarily in two main clusters: one to the north and one to the east-centre of the tenement package. The east-central cluster appears particularly dense with mixed high (red), moderate (yellow and green), and low (cyan) grade samples, suggesting a zone of variable but significant gold mineralisation. A diagonal **black line** traversing the map likely represents a road or infrastructure corridor.

The overall pattern suggests a **north-northwest trending corridor** of gold-bearing mullock heaps broadly following the tenement boundaries, consistent with mineralisation along a greenstone belt structure typical of the Murchison goldfield.

- **Legend title:** "Mullock Sampling ppm Au"

  - 1.6 – 123

## Research Value
This figure provides a rapid spatial assessment of **historical gold grades in mullock heaps** across a substantial tenement package near Coe, Western Australia, allowing identification of priority targets for follow-up exploration or re-processing. The clustering of high-grade (>1.6 ppm, up to 123 ppm Au) mullock samples in the north and east-central portions of the tenure indicates that these areas hosted significant historical workings and retain strong gold anomalism warranting modern systematic evaluation. Combined with the proximity to known workings such as Great Cuchulainn Pit and South Cuchulainn, the data supports targeting along what is likely the **Coe Domain greenstone belt**, a well-known Archaean gold-bearing sequence in the Murchison Province.
